4988: Postprocessual and Interpretive Archaeologies
4989: 
4990: ‘Postprocessual archaeology’ grew out of a very specific context that it
4991: is important to understand. Part of that context within the human
4992: sciences as a whole – developments in structuralism and Marxism – was
4993: presented in the last chapter. Now, I want to look briefly at
4994: developments within archaeology in the 1980s.
4995: 
4996: In the late 1970s and early 1980s, a number of archaeologists became
4997: increasingly dissatisfied with the direction archaeology was taking. In
4998: particular, they pointed to the need to address cognitive factors, the
4999: difficulties of positivist epistemology, and the problems with
5000: developing middle-range theory – issues dealt with in chapters 3 to 6.
5001: 
5002: One such archaeologist was Ian Hodder, and his intellectual development
5003: epitomizes this shift in many ways. Hodder’s early work was very much
5004: within a processual mould. Hodder was heavily influenced by the ‘New
5005: Geography’ and the work of New Archaeologist David Clarke on spatial
5006: models in archaeology. Hodder used statistics and computer simulation to
5007: develop a series of spatial models, particularly relating to trade,
5008: markets and urbanization in Iron Age and Roman Britain. This period was
5009: seen very much as an evolving system, with trade and urbanism being
5010: linked as part of an overall process of ‘Romanization’.
5011: 
5012: But as the research progressed Hodder became more and more doubtful that
5013: such models and simulations did really ‘test’ or ‘prove’ anything. The
5014: same pattern or trace in the archaeological record, for example a
5015: pottery distribution or network of urban centres, could be produced by a
5016: range of different simulated processes. Therefore, a given pattern in
5017: the archaeological record could be satisfactorily interpreted or
5018: explained in different ways, with reference to a number of different
5019: possible processes. Hodder felt that there was no way to test absolutely
5020: between these alternatives. This is often referred to in subsequent
5021: literature as a problem of equifinality.
5022: 
5023: A reading of Hodder and Orton’s Spatial Analysis in Archaeology,
5024: published in 1976, shows this shift. In case after case – attempting to
5025: simulate trade patterns and settlement systems using computers – Hodder
5026: and Orton showed how difficult it was to ‘prove’ or ‘test’ anything
5027: conclusively.
5028: 
5029: A good example was the study of goods used in prehistoric trade. Goods
5030: such as obsidian flakes or polished greenstone axes were often found on
5031: sites thousands of kilometres from the original source of the raw
5032: material. Such finds clearly represented trade or contact in some form,
5033: but ambitious New Archaeologists wanted to go further than this. Renfrew
5034: and others had suggested that different forms of trade would leave
5035: different traces in the archaeological record. If, for example,
5036: ‘down-the-line’ exchange was occurring, with community A collecting the
5037: material at source before giving community B half, then community B
5038: keeping half and handing half on … then different quantities of traded
5039: material would be present in site assemblages than if everyone was going
5040: back to source and making their own collation of material.
5041: 
5042: Hodder found that if ‘down-the-line’, directional or other modes of
5043: exchange were modelled by computer simulation, similar rather than
5044: different curves were produced. Different forms of process left the same
5045: archaeological trace: in other words, they were equifinal (figure 7.1).
5046: 
5047: Lessons also came out of Hodder’s ethnoarchaeological work. Hodder
5048: realized that however many archaeological facts you collected on a
5049: computer, the only way to get a handle on what they meant in terms of
5050: past activities is to look at the relationship between pattern and
5051: process in the present. This, of course, was a similar and parallel
5052: observation to Binford’s perception of the problems underlying the
5053: ‘Mousterian debate’ as discussed in chapter 4. Like Binford, Hodder
5054: decided to turn to studies of ‘archaeology in the present’ to try to
5055: establish correlations between behaviour in the present and
5056: archaeological patterning. So Hodder went out to East Africa to examine,
5057: among other themes, how real living ‘cultures’ could be mapped
5058: archaeologically, what factors affected refuse disposal, and so on.
5059: 
5060: What Hodder found, again in a variety of instances, was that to
5061: understand patterning on the ground it was necessary to refer to
5062: people’s attitudes and beliefs. We looked at Hodder’s work with the Nuba
5063: in chapter 4. This led him to:
5064: 
5065: 1 a rejection of Binford’s confidence in the ability of middle-range
5066: theory to act as a neutral arbiter between different explanations;
5067: 
5068: 2 a belief in the importance of people’s thoughts and symbolism, and
5069: with this a belief that cultures could not be viewed purely as adapting
5070: to an external environment: ‘their’ view of ‘their’ world was important;
5071: 
5072: 3 a belief that material culture was actively manipulated by people;
5073: that is, that people used things in different ways as part of particular
5074: social strategies, rather than material things being a passive
5075: reflection of a set of rules.
5076: 
5077: Figure 7.1 The results of Hodder and Orton’s simulation exercise showing
5078: that ‘different spatial processes can produce very similar fall-off
5079: curves’, implying that ‘this advises great caution in any attempt at
5080: interpretation’ (Hodder and Orton 1976: figure 5.35 and p. 145
5081: 
5082: [c07_image001.jpg]
5083: 
5084: How might we do archaeology in this way? Several theories dealing with
5085: ‘thoughts’, ‘ideologies’ or ‘mind’ were looked at in the previous
5086: chapter. In the early 1980s a new generation of students, many of whom
5087: were studying with Hodder at Cambridge, and others with Mark Leone as
5088: part of the Archaeology in Annapolis project, turned to these theories.
5089: Many turned to structuralism as a way into understanding the mind.
5090: Others ‘read around’ Marxist and neo-Marxist texts and in particular
5091: critical theory. Others were influenced by feminist thinking, while
5092: others still looked at the writings of figures like Clifford Geertz in
5093: ‘interpretative anthropology’. Together, these very diverse strands of
5094: thought coalesced into a very loose cluster of traditions that came to
5095: be known by both supporters and critics as ‘postprocessual’ or
5096: ‘interpretive’ archaeology.
5097: 
5098: Postprocessual Archaeology
5099: 
5100: There is no such thing as a ‘postprocessual archaeologist’. Whenever I
5101: see the phrase ‘the postprocessualists’ used in the archaeological
5102: literature I expect some out-of-date and usually hostile
5103: over-generalization of theoretical attitudes to follow, and I am rarely
5104: disappointed. Just as New Archaeology was actually a very diverse set of
5105: concerns and ideas that coalesced around certain slogans, so the
5106: catch-all term ‘postprocessual’ conceals a great diversity of viewpoints
5107: and traditions. Indeed, ‘postprocessualism’ is better characterized as a
5108: historical term, referring, like New Archaeology, to a set of themes
5109: characteristic of a specific period that have evolved into a much
5110: broader and very diverse set of concerns and approaches.
5111: 
5112: So instead I shall try to characterize postprocessual thinking as a
5113: historical moment, a crystallization of concerns raised in the 1980s and
5114: 1990s, that has gone on to inform archaeological thought as a whole. I
5115: shall do so in the form of eight key statements that convey the flavour
5116: of postprocessual traditions, a way of looking at and thinking about the
5117: world. They should also indicate the debt owed to the intellectual
5118: movements described in the previous chapter.
5119: 
5120: (1) We reject a positivist view of science and the theory/data split.
5121: The data are always theory-laden. Postprocessualists rejected the claims
5122: of Science as a unique form of knowledge, for the reasons advanced in
5123: chapter 3. Generally, they aligned themselves with other non-positivist
5124: conceptions of what science is or does, particularly social
5125: constructivism whether in its ‘strong’ or ‘weak’ forms.
5126: 
5127: Or to put it another way, postprocessualists did not argue that ‘we
5128: should not test things’; rather, they suggested that in practice neither
5129: scientists nor archaeologists ever test things in ways that satisfy
5130: positivist criteria. They pointed out, for example, that Renfrew’s
5131: ‘testing’ of his territorial model of megaliths in figure 2.7 was no
5132: such thing; the territories delineated are far from uniform, with many
5133: megaliths centred within totally unconvincing territories;
5134: archaeologists use a much looser set of criteria to agree (or disagree)
5135: that Renfrew’s is a convincing argument. Postprocessualists suggested
5136: that archaeologists can never confront theory and data as two clearly
5137: independent and opposable categories; instead, we see data through a
5138: cloud of theory (figure 7.2; contrast with figure 4.1).
5139: 
5140: Figure 7.2 The relationship of theory and data in postprocessual
5141: archaeology; data exist, but are perceived fuzzily through a cloud of
5142: theory (contrast with figure 4.1)
5143: 
5144: [c07_image002.jpg]
5145: 
5146: (2) Interpretation is always hermeneutic. This is a variant on
5147: Proposition (1). Hermeneutics is the study of meanings. When
5148: archaeologists interpret things, it was argued, they do this by
5149: assigning meanings to them, meanings that it is assumed were also in the
5150: minds of the ancient peoples who made and used them.
5151: 
5152: Postprocessualists argued that all archaeologists do this whether they
5153: overtly admit it or not. When the Italian philosopher Croce, cited in
5154: the last chapter, talks about simply describing and classifying and
5155: going no further, the response is that even these simple actions of
5156: description and classification are making assumptions about what was in
5157: the heads of Neolithic Sicilians. Postprocessualists deconstructed
5158: accounts of ‘scientific’ testing to show that even Binford and others
5159: implicitly assume meanings and values in the minds of ancient peoples.
5160: Hodder, for example, looked at the day-to-day process of reasoning
5161: during an archaeological excavation and argued that this is always a
5162: ‘hermeneutic circle’ regardless of whether or not the excavators
5163: consider themselves theorists, a proposition that he has developed and
5164: explored in the ongoing project at Çatal Höyük.
5165: 
5166: (3) We reject the opposition between material and ideal. We have seen
5167: how normative and culture-historical approaches were rejected by
5168: processual archaeology as idealist, and how processual archaeology
5169: introduced a materialist emphasis. We have also looked at the idealist
5170: approach taken by structuralism, and at how Marxism moved away from a
5171: purely materialist base.
5172: 
5173: Postprocessualists claimed that we should reject the whole opposition
5174: between material and ideal in the first place. A good example is the
5175: idea of landscape. On the one hand, a materialist view of landscape
5176: tends to stress how it may be seen in terms of a set of resources, for
5177: example for huntergatherers or early farming groups. This leads one to
5178: turn, for example, to optimal foraging theory and other economic models
5179: for an understanding of how people exploited the landscape ‘rationally’.
5180: 
5181: Postprocessualists suggested that landscapes are always viewed in
5182: different ways by different peoples. They rejected the ‘rational’ view
5183: of ‘landscapeas-set-of-resources’ as that of our own society and one
5184: that is ideologically loaded in its own way, loaded towards ideas of
5185: commodity and exploitation found in our own society. They suggested
5186: instead that ancient peoples would have had different views of what was
5187: ‘real’ in that landscape.
5188: 
5189: On the other hand, an exclusively idealist view of landscape does not
5190: work either. Postprocessualists stressed that such an understanding of
5191: landscape was not formed in the abstract, as an ethereal or detached set
5192: of preconceptions – that the way people moved around and used that
5193: landscape affected their understanding of it. They argued that ancient
5194: people’s understanding of a landscape was not just a set of thoughts
5195: they happened to possess; everyday movements through the landscape,
5196: farming, domestic activities were all media through which understanding
5197: of the landscape was perpetuated and transformed.
5198: 
5199: Postprocessual archaeology therefore developed an interest in practice
5200: for example the everyday rhythms of moving around a village or a
5201: landscape, the repetitive actions of daily rounds. It stressed that
5202: thoughts and ideas do not arise in the abstract, but through these
5203: practical activities and rounds.
5204: 
5205: (4) We need to look at thoughts and values in the past. The most
5206: coherent example of this proposition was Hodder’s advocacy of R. G.
5207: Collingwood’s position of historical idealism. Collingwood was a
5208: philosopher by training, though he also practised history and
5209: archaeology. He argued that, in practice, historians always try to
5210: rethink the thoughts of the past. Take for example a classic historical
5211: question: why did the British naval leader Lord Nelson not change into
5212: plain dress before the naval battle of Trafalgar? (In the battle
5213: Nelson’s medals and gaudy appearance made him a natural target for
5214: snipers, and he was fatally wounded.) Historians, says Collingwood,
5215: explain that it was considered dishonourable for a commanding officer to
5216: go below deck once battle had commenced; and that Nelson had no such
5217: opportunity before the first shots were fired. So, argues Collingwood,
5218: when historians ‘understand’ or ‘explain’ Nelson’s actions, they explore
5219: by definition the culture and assumptions of a British naval officer,
5220: they rethink his thoughts – in other words, they use empathy.
5221: 
5222: Hodder, following Collingwood, argued that archaeologists do this all
5223: the time no matter what theoretical pose they claim to adopt. When, for
5224: example, traditional archaeologists ‘explained’ the placement of Roman
5225: forts on the northern frontier of Britain, they did so in terms of an
5226: evolving military and political strategy – in other words, they ended up
5227: trying to rethink the past thoughts of Roman commanders and emperors.
5228: Again, the argument is that all archaeologists actually practise
5229: empathetic thinking whether they admit it or not.
5230: 
5231: (5) The individual is active. Postprocessualists disliked the way they
5232: felt ‘the individual’ is lost in much archaeological theory.
5233: Individuals, they complained, were just pawns in some set of normative
5234: rules or adaptive systems or set of deep structures. They argued that
5235: all these different views of the world portrayed people as passive dupes
5236: who blindly follow social rules.
5237: 
5238: Instead, postprocessualists stressed agency. Agency is a term used to
5239: refer to the active strategies of individuals. In this view, women and
5240: men are not passively duped by the system around them. Some
5241: archaeologists borrowed the idea of a recursive relationship between
5242: structure and agency from the sociologist Anthony Giddens. Giddens
5243: suggested that there are social rules in the world around us, but that
5244: people understand these rules and manipulate them creatively rather than
5245: follow them passively. In so doing, they reinforce or alternatively
5246: transform the structure itself – the relationship is therefore
5247: recursive, or back-and-forth. Giddens’s ideas on structure and agency
5248: are often referred to as structuration theory.
5249: 
5250: A different way of saying a similar thing was borrowed from the work of
5251: the French anthropologist Pierre Bourdieu. Bourdieu was reacting to the
5252: orthodox structuralist anthropology of his time, in which he felt humans
5253: were simply seen as passively acting out a set of structural rules.
5254: Bourdieu showed how in different ethnographic situations different
5255: actors had their own ideas about social rules and situations. He argued
5256: that we needed a theory of practice – a theory of how individual social
5257: actors actually practised living in, reproducing and transforming the
5258: culture around them.
5259: 
5260: In terms of interpreting the archaeological record, then, archaeologists
5261: need to look at rules that are not just followed, but are creatively
5262: manipulated by social actors. A good example of such manipulation comes
5263: from an unpublished anecdote from Hodder’s work with the Nuba. In one
5264: area, there was a cultural belief that women were associated with the
5265: ‘inside’ of the domestic compound, and with the hearth; also, that women
5266: were ‘polluting’. Women were therefore enjoined to dump the ash from the
5267: hearth inside the compound, rather than outside where it would pollute
5268: the male sphere. But Hodder once saw a woman self-consciously gathering
5269: up the hearth ash and dumping it outside, some way from the compound
5270: where all the men could see it. Now in so doing she was acknowledging
5271: that the rule existed, but deliberately breaking it. We cannot
5272: understand her action and the archaeological signature it produced
5273: without (a) understanding the cultural system of rules, but (b)
5274: understanding her position towards those rules.
5275: 
5276: (6) Material culture is like a text. How do we understand the meanings
5277: of material culture? Think about the way you read any written text:
5278: 
5279: (a) A text can mean different things to different people, and different
5280: people can read texts in different ways.
5281: 
5282: (b) These meanings can be actively manipulated. Individuals do this with
5283: material culture in obvious and trivial ways, most obviously with
5284: clothes (we can define the formality of a meeting according to whether
5285: we put on a skirt or a jacket and tie, for example).
5286: 
5287: (c) Such manipulation is often implicit and unspoken. Readers do not
5288: consciously think through grammatical rules as they read a text;
5289: similarly, we don’t consciously think through rules governing material
5290: culture.
5291: 
5292: Consider for example the action of coming into a room without knocking;
5293: we might consider somebody doing this to be ‘impolite’. At the same time
5294: we might deliberately break the rule, for example not knocking if we
5295: thought the room was ‘our’ space and we wanted to make that point to the
5296: person currently occupying it. Underlying this grammar of actions are
5297: assumptions and cultural values about the nature of space, rules of
5298: ‘privacy’, respect for the individual, and so on. We know what those
5299: rules are in our own society, and we manipulate them, though we do not
5300: consciously articulate them – we would not give a lecture on cultural
5301: anthropology to someone breaking that code, we would simply ask them to
5302: be more polite in future. Similar things go on with ancient material
5303: culture in general. Postprocessualists pointed to ethnoarchaeological
5304: studies in which the meanings of space in houses and compounds are
5305: rarely overtly discussed, for example, but which are manipulated by
5306: social actors with reference to certain rules.
5307: 
5308: (d) If the meanings of material culture are really this complex, then
5309: its different meanings can never be definitively or finally tied down in
5310: some final ‘conclusion’, a single all-embracing analysis. Therefore
5311: there can be no ‘final’ reading of a text – each generation, and even
5312: each individual, brings fresh readings to a Shakespeare play, and each
5313: reading has its own validity. There can, in this view, also be no single
5314: ‘right’ or ‘wrong’ reading of a text in any absolute sense. A text can
5315: always be deconstructed and shown to contain hidden meanings opposite to
5316: those on the surface, as we have seen in the last chapter. Similarly,
5317: the meanings of a pottery design or a burial rite can never be finally
5318: tied down; there is no one ‘right’ or ‘wrong’ reading.
5319: Postprocessualists therefore encouraged experimentation with multiple
5320: interpretations, and denied the necessity of coming up with one final
5321: conclusion that explains ‘everything’.
5322: 
5323: (e) The meanings of a text are outside the control of its author – the
5324: wellknown phrase coined by poststructuralists: the ‘death of the
5325: author’. If a text is open to multiple interpretations, some of these
5326: may be quite at odds with the reading that the author might consciously
5327: prefer. We cannot therefore refer to the author’s conscious intention in
5328: producing a text, whether literary or archaeological, as the only
5329: correct reading.
5330: 
5331: (7) We have to look at context. How do archaeologists get at different
5332: meanings? Through, postprocessualists argued, looking at the context of
5333: the artefact or the practice being discussed. Let’s return to our
5334: burial. We see that a particular grave good, say an axe, has a
5335: particular meaning through its context – where it is buried in the
5336: grave, the person it is buried with, the objects with which it is
5337: associated. We then widen that context and look at other axes in other
5338: graves from the same cemetery. We find that the axe is used differently
5339: in different contexts – it is associated with different assemblages, or
5340: is placed in a different position in the graves of women, men and
5341: children. We infer differences in meaning from these differences in
5342: context.
5343: 
5344: Archaeologists might then expand the argument contextually by looking at
5345: axes within our ancient culture generally – whether they are deposited
5346: in other contexts such as ‘domestic’ or ‘refuse’, or by looking at how
5347: they are used in domestic arenas. Gradually, a whole web of associations
5348: and placements for the axes is built up.
5349: 
5350: (8) The meanings we produce are always in the political present, and
5351: always have political resonance. Interpreting the past is always a
5352: political act. If scientific neutrality is argued to be a myth, then
5353: statements about the past are never cool objective judgements detached
5354: from the real world. They are always made here, in the present, with all
5355: its heady and complicated, jumbled mixture of political and moral
5356: judgements.
5357: 
5358: Note that this does not mean that individual archaeologists are
5359: insincere in their attempts to be objective. If the meanings of a text
5360: are outside the control of its author, then readings of a text can
5361: proliferate in ways never consciously intended by its ‘author’. It has
5362: been argued for example that some New Archaeologists working on Native
5363: American sites stressed that the value of their work lay in their
5364: ability to use this material to generate cross-cultural generalizations,
5365: that is statements that were true about all human populations in all
5366: places. By doing so, some argued, these archaeologists implicitly
5367: devalued the importance of looking at Native American tradition in its
5368: own right – the implicit message could be read as ‘the only valid way to
5369: look at this archaeology is to stress its relevance to white people’.
5370: Such a ‘reading’ is not to argue that such archaeologists were being
5371: consciously racist; indeed, many such archaeologists were active
5372: campaigners for Native American rights.
5373: 
5374: Case Studies: Rock Art and Medieval Houses
5375: 
5376: In Material Culture and Text: The Art of Ambiguity Chris Tilley (1991)
5377: explored the interpretation of a group of rock carvings in Nämforsen in
5378: Sweden (figure 7.3). At this site, figures and motifs were pecked into
5379: the stones in the third millennium BC. This material was recorded by the
5380: Swedish archaeologist Hallström around the turn of the century; many of
5381: the carvings have now been destroyed. Tilley does not choose to organize
5382: his analysis in an orthodox way, with chapters on approach preceding
5383: those on material. He starts instead with a consideration of the
5384: material itself and of Hallström’s recording of it, moves on to develop
5385: a grammar of design form, and then tries to understand that form through
5386: its ‘structural logic’. Having developed such a logic and explored
5387: different ways in which that might relate to the communities that
5388: produced the figures, Tilley moves on to look at different ethnographic
5389: parallels, both of a direct historical nature with modern groups such as
5390: the Saami and of other traditions such as the Australian Aborigines.
5391: 
5392: Thus, two-thirds of the way in, we have reached the critical point of
5393: the book. Tilley has given us an interpretation of the meaning of the
5394: carvings. But in the final third of the book, he proceeds to deconstruct
5395: his own interpretation. Tilley refuses to give the reader one ‘pat’
5396: answer to the meaning of the carvings: ‘the point I am really trying to
5397: make is that these rock carvings invite a response from us. … There is
5398: no fixed meaning and we must remember that images cannot in fact be
5399: reduced to words, “read”… I do not present a proper conclusion because
5400: this is an impossibility’ (1991: 172). Tilley, then, has given us one
5401: final answer, but has then deconstructed that answer and shown that it
5402: is not final at all.
5403: 
5404: A second example comes from my own work: the interpretation of the rural
5405: house in the later medieval period in England (c. ad 1350–1530).
5406: 
5407: In southern and eastern England, thousands of ordinary houses built
5408: during this period still stand and are used as modern homes, albeit
5409: usually heavily modified. Their plan varies, but invariably has one
5410: central element: a large hall or central room, open to the roof. This
5411: room was usually heated by a central open hearth, the smoke rising to
5412: and dispersing in the rafters and thatched roof (figure 7.4).
5413: 
5414: Figure 7.3 (a) Carvings from Nämforsen, (b) part of Tilley’s structural
5415: scheme for interpreting the carvings
5416: 
5417: [c07_image003.jpg][c07_image004.jpg]
5418: 
5419: Figure 7.4 A medieval hall (after Johnson 1989: figure 2)
5420: 
5421: [c07_image005.jpg]
5422: 
5423: Traditional archaeologists and historians had done much work identifying
5424: these buildings and assigning dates to them, often on the basis of
5425: technological, stylistic and typological features. We knew much also
5426: about who lived in them. Such houses had been built by socially middling
5427: groups – tenant farmers, often combining farming with rural industry,
5428: particularly cloth production. We had analysed the technology and
5429: building practices embodied in the houses – the carpentry and framing
5430: techniques. Much work had also been done on the economic background to
5431: these houses – how and why such non-élite groups could afford a
5432: substantial, permanent house for the first time.
5433: 
5434: These questions were interesting, but I wanted to ask: why was the hall
5435: the form that it was and what meanings did that form have for those that
5436: built and lived in it? Many previous writers had claimed that the hall
5437: was simply a matter of common sense: the openness to the roof space was
5438: needed to allow smoke from the open fire to disperse among the rafters.
5439: I felt this was not the whole story – chimneys had been known in this
5440: society and used at upper social levels in buildings like medieval
5441: castles and palaces for several centuries. The peasants occupying such
5442: houses were wealthy enough to afford chimney-stacks if they so desired;
5443: they simply chose not to have them.
5444: 
5445: The hall was an open space, but was divided into upper and lower ends by
5446: a series of architectural features. The doors were symmetrically placed
5447: at the colder, draughtier, ‘lower’ end, which also offered access to
5448: ‘service’ rooms. At the other, ‘upper’ end there were often fittings for
5449: a raised chair or bench to seat the master and wife; the area was lit by
5450: a large window. The bay in the upper end was often one rather longer
5451: than that in the lower; the bench at the upper end was fixed by pegs to
5452: the wall.
5453: 
5454: It was easy enough to see the hall, then, as a piece of ideology. Upper
5455: and lower ends reflected upper and lower social divisions. The hall
5456: presented the social divisions of the household as fixed and immutable
5457: through the architecture. The master and wife occupied the upper end and
5458: the chamber beyond; servants worked in and around the lower end. At
5459: mealtimes, the hall acted as a microcosm of the social order. Everyone
5460: ate in the same place, stressing the household as community, but at
5461: different ends, stressing differences in social status between different
5462: elements of that household.
5463: 
5464: But the interpretation of the hall could not be this simple. There were
5465: other divisions in the late medieval household that cut across the
5466: upper/lower divide and could not be fitted into a single pattern so
5467: easily. Women occupied important and powerful economic positions within
5468: the late medieval household: they controlled brewing and dairying
5469: activities, and were identified in idealized religious and political
5470: texts with the ‘domestic’ side of the house as opposed to the ‘public’
5471: outside sphere of the roads and fields. There was tension and conflict,
5472: then, between the important economic position and everyday routine of
5473: women’s lives and the formal ideology of patriarchy. So I tried to
5474: explore how other groups, women and servants in particular, may have had
5475: different ‘readings’ of the same space. These different ‘readings’ were
5476: rarely overtly articulated; they were, rather, implicit.
5477: 
5478: I also tried to look at the context of the open hall by looking at other
5479: kinds of use of space in the same period. First, I looked at fields, and
5480: argued that just as the open hall was open to all but in fact
5481: subdivided, so it was in the ‘open field’: medieval fields were often
5482: unbounded and farmed cooperatively, but divided into strips. Then I
5483: looked at churches, and suggested that ritual space was also divided,
5484: between the nave and chancel of the parish church. So I was arguing that
5485: a series of common spatial metaphors ran across ordinary people’s
5486: experiences of the world around them, that ran between the domestic, the
5487: agrarian and the religious.
5488: 
5489: The whole debate between archaeologists over the meanings of the open
5490: hall could not be seen independently of its cultural and political
5491: context. The ‘vernacular house’ occupies a central place in images of
5492: rural England. In particular, these images implied continuity with an
5493: essential, unchanging rural past of ‘old England’ in which ‘the English’
5494: are seen as a traditional, conservative organic community. In the same
5495: year that I published my work, Conservative Prime Minister John Major
5496: linked ‘old England’ to a vision of ‘British’ continuity when he
5497: exclaimed that:
5498: 
5499: Fifty years from now Britain will still be the country of long shadows
5500: on county grounds, warm beer, invincible green suburbs, dog lovers and
5501: pools fillers and – as George Orwell said – ‘old maids cycling to holy
5502: communion through the morning mist’ and – if we get our way –
5503: Shakespeare still read even in school. Britain will survive unamendable
5504: in all essentials. (John Major, speech to Conservative Group for Europe,
5505: 22 April 1993)
5506: 
5507: In placing these houses within a context of profound rural crisis and
5508: change, and emphasizing their different meanings to different elements
5509: of the household, I was implicitly commenting negatively on that
5510: profoundly conservative image of the English countryside and
5511: ‘Englishness’ in general. This political comment was unavoidable; it was
5512: there whether I liked it or not. At the time, it wasn’t my conscious
5513: intention to comment implicitly or explicitly on John Major’s views, but
5514: a subsequent reading of ‘my’ work in such a light would nevertheless be
5515: a perfectly valid one – though it is one of several possible readings.
5516: 
5517: Phenomenology
5518: 
5519: Stop – I’ve lots of questions already! Can I drag you back to Point Six?
5520: Surely there are lots of problems with this analogy with text.
5521: 
5522: Yes, there are. I’ve emphasized text here because it is a good
5523: introduction to the underlying ideas. We all read texts, and can all
5524: grasp the analogy quickly. The analogy with text has a heuristic value –
5525: in other words, it helps us learn.
5526: 
5527: However, many archaeologists have argued within the postprocessual
5528: tradition that we should get away from this analogy, that it is
5529: fundamentally limited. In particular, it can be argued that material
5530: things convey meaning in fundamentally dissimilar ways to texts.
5531: 
5532: Material things are exactly that – physical objects. Their properties
5533: are, in many ways, very different from words. Material things can be
5534: functional in ways that words are not. Further, the sign is rarely
5535: arbitrary as it is in structuralism. The material world, then, is not
5536: reducible to language, and more broadly, the material world is a complex
5537: thing that is not easily understood in its own right. Perhaps, in that
5538: case, archaeologists should not talk about ‘meanings’, but rather more
5539: broadly about ‘understanding’ and ‘experience’.
5540: 
5541: Thinking about the material world in this way has led to an interest in
5542: the last decade in questions of materiality, from both within and
5543: outside postprocessual traditions. How, precisely, do objects come to
5544: carry meanings, and how do the properties of the material world and of
5545: the social world interact? Should we even make this distinction in the
5546: first place? I will return to materiality, along with agency, in the
5547: concluding chapters of this book, as an area of common interest and
5548: concern from different theoretical viewpoints.
5549: 
5550: Perception that the textual analogy was limited in this regard led
5551: postprocessual archaeologists to explore other ways of thinking about
5552: how we might explore meaning through archaeological evidence. One such
5553: method or philosophy is that of phenomenology:
5554: 
5555: Phenomenology involves the study and description of phenomena. A
5556: phenomenon is any entity (thing or event) which presents itself as a
5557: subject in the world. So the central concern is with the
5558: conceptualization of subjectobject relations. It involves a description
5559: of things as they are experienced in the world by a human subject …
5560: Processes of ordinary human description are of central significance:
5561: seeing, hearing, touching, smelling, tasting. Phenomenologists try to
5562: ground their descriptions of the social and material world in the manner
5563: in which people think and feel about it rather than in an abstract
5564: manner. (Tilley 2005, 201–2).
5565: 
5566: The philosophical origins of phenomenology go back to German
5567: Romanticism, but from that point branched out into a number of different
5568: traditions, including the ‘phenomenological sociology’ of Alfred Schutz.
5569: Archaeologists have borrowed particularly from the thinking of Husserl,
5570: as refracted through the work of Heidegger, Merleau-Ponty and Sartre.
5571: Instead of thinking about monuments as texts being read in different
5572: ways, archaeologists have talked about how people move through
5573: monuments, what they can or cannot see from different points, how the
5574: physical experience of the monument affects its perception.
5575: 
5576: Phenomenology has been applied particularly to the study of landscape
5577: and to monuments in the landscape. Classic case studies include studies
5578: of the megalithic monuments of Wales, the landscape settings of the
5579: great monuments of prehistoric Wessex such as Stonehenge and Avebury,
5580: and the Neolithic Dorset Cursus. In all these cases, phenomenologists
5581: direct attention away from the map and the plan view, which, it is
5582: claimed, offers only a top-down, scientific, Cartesian view divorced
5583: from everyday experience. Instead, phenomenologists try to describe what
5584: it is like in human terms to walk along, around, through these
5585: monuments, what one sees and hears at different points, how one’s
5586: experience is affected by temporal rhythm (the order in which one sees
5587: things, the appearance of sudden ‘surprises’ over the horizon).
5588: 
5589: One of the advantages of phenomenology is that in its stress on the body
5590: and on bodily movement, it stresses the falsity of the opposition
5591: between material and ideal mentioned above. The body is undeniably real
5592: and physical – as Shakespeare said, ‘there was never yet
5593: philosopher/That could endure the toothache patiently’ – and yet at the
5594: same time undeniably constructed according to certain socially specific
5595: and variable ideas – different societies have different ideas of the
5596: individual, of gender, of how the body works.
5597: 
5598: A second advantage is that, for all its reputation as an obscure and
5599: esoteric philosophy, phenomenology directs the attention of
5600: archaeologists to aspects of experience which are (arguably) both
5601: accessible and observable. What objects and landscapes look like, the
5602: sensations of touch, taste and smell, are, on the face of it, things
5603: that can be talked about quite readily by archaeologists. Archaeologists
5604: do not dig up factional competition or differential access to resources
5605: or the rise of the Roman Empire; these processes are actually quite
5606: indirectly inferred from the evidence that we have. On the other hand,
5607: we do know how dark it is inside a Neolithic chambered tomb, we can
5608: experience how sound travels or fails to travel between different
5609: spaces, or how difficult it is to climb to the top of a castle wall.
5610: 
5611: Nevertheless, phenomenology is a particularly controversial as well as
5612: vibrant area of archaeological theory. Phenomenology started out as a
5613: philosophical questioning of how we might understand subjective human
5614: experience. In practice, however, practical applications of
5615: phenomenology to archaeology have to assume some kind of empathetic
5616: unity to human experience, an assumption that ‘our’ experience now has a
5617: direct link to ‘their’ experience in the past, in order to be
5618: convincing. In the examples given above, how do we know how ‘darkness’
5619: was experienced in the Neolithic? How can we assume that sounds were
5620: experienced or given meaning in the same way? Doesn’t the difficulty of
5621: climbing a castle wall depend on one’s age and gender, or whether one
5622: has a disability? The human subjects described by phenomenologists often
5623: appear, when one thinks about it, to be quite culturally specific. The
5624: people depicted by some phenomenologists wandering around the landscape
5625: are often solitary; they are not carrying children; they are free from
5626: impairment or disability. In short, critics have rather cruelly but
5627: perhaps accurately suggested, they are curiously like some of the
5628: archaeologists doing the analysis.
5629: 
5630: A second development from a dissatisfaction with texts is to think more
5631: deeply about semiotics. If structuralist and poststructuralists models
5632: of the material world are limited or flawed, perhaps there are other
5633: theories to which archaeologists can turn.
5634: 
5635: Semiotics is the study of signs. It can be argued that the world around
5636: us is, fundamentally, made up of signs as part of a total signifying
5637: system. Recently, the work of the philosopher Charles Peirce has gained
5638: in popularity among archaeologists. Peirce saw the sign, the object, and
5639: the interpretant in a triangular relationship, and developed a complex
5640: typology of different kinds of signs. These included the the icon (where
5641: there is a formal relationship between sign and object), the index
5642: (where there is a relationship of contiguity), and the symbol (where the
5643: relationship is arbitrary, as Saussure argued for language).
5644: 
5645: A Peircean semiotics is highly complex. However, it has value as a way
5646: of thinking about the world in terms of signs. Semiotics, then,
5647: addresses the need to understand meanings without resorting to the
5648: disputed textual analogy of structuralism.
5649: 
5650: So postprocessualists denied the value of testing. Doesn’t this mean
5651: they were open to the charge of relativism?
5652: 
5653: Well, to repeat, they didn’t ‘deny its value’; they claimed that, in
5654: practice, no archaeologist whatever their theoretical stripe actually
5655: tests theory against ‘raw data’ in the way described by positivists.
5656: However, the identification of postprocessualism with relativism by its
5657: critics led to a quite bad-tempered debate in the 1990s which
5658: occasionally resurfaces today.
5659: 
5660: I will discuss this issue further in chapter 12; but as an interim
5661: statement, we can note a softening on all sides. On the one hand, we
5662: have noted how within processual traditions, a hard-line, positivist
5663: language of ‘testing’ has been softened over the decades, to an
5664: insistence that archaeologists’ ideas about the past must be evaluated
5665: against the evidence, and that archaeologists should do this in a robust
5666: and rigorous manner. On the other hand, in part as a response to the
5667: repeated criticisms of the 1990s, most archaeologists associated with
5668: postprocessual ideas take pains to stress that interpretations of the
5669: past are at the very least constrained by reference to the evidence.
5670: 
5671: In my view, the critical question is not ‘should interpretations be
5672: evaluated against the evidence?’; of course they should. Rather, the key
5673: question is, ‘what, in practice, are the methods by which archaeologists
5674: do so? And how can those methods be strengthened?’. I have tried to
5675: address this question in my work on traditional landscape archaeology,
5676: arguing for a series of methods to bring theory and data more closely
5677: and rigorously together.
5678: 
5679: Well we certainly can’t ‘test’ people’s thoughts. How can we ever
5680: understand what people were really thinking? Not only are they now dead,
5681: but their culture and values were almost certainly very, very different
5682: to our own. It’s striking that the two examples you chose (Nelson and
5683: Roman forts) were from military history.
5684: 
5685: It is very difficult – but archaeology is very difficult. It’s certainly
5686: true that to my mind many of the most exciting and fruitful
5687: ‘postprocessual’ case studies have been done in historical archaeology,
5688: where there is plenty of documentary and ethnohistoric data to bring to
5689: bear on questions of mentality (see chapter 11). This shows the
5690: importance of context – of having plenty of contextual information to
5691: work through. It is also true, as we have seen, that archaeologists
5692: influenced by phenomenology have preferred to talk about ‘lived
5693: experience’ and ‘understanding’ rather than specific cultural ‘meaning’.
5694: 
5695: A more complex answer to your question is that we may never be able to
5696: grasp fully the content of ancient belief systems: we will never ‘know’
5697: that this figurine was a depiction of that goddess, or ‘know’ the
5698: stories and myths told about the goddess using the figurine as a prop.
5699: But we can move towards an anthropological description of what a
5700: figurine or architectural space might have meant at a deeper level, for
5701: example by noting that ‘female’ figurines are found on the left side of
5702: a temple while male figurines are on the right. Anthropologists do this
5703: as standard practice, for example moving beyond the overt stories told
5704: by a community or folk tradition to inferences about the ‘underlying
5705: meanings’ of those stories – underlying meanings that might be overtly
5706: denied by the community in question.
5707: 
5708: So, to give an example, we might take the story of Little Red Riding
5709: Hood and interpret it in terms of ideas about gender and fears of
5710: adolescent sexuality in Western culture. With prehistoric or ancient
5711: cultures, we will never know the stories, but we can use the
5712: archaeological material and its contextual associations to get some
5713: sense of what such underlying ideas might be.
5714: 
5715: I’ve heard an awful lot about ‘the postmodernists’ lately. Is
5716: postprocessual archaeology the same thing as postmodernism?
5717: 
5718: Oh dear, I was hoping you wouldn’t ask that. This is a thorny issue that
5719: had better wait till chapter 12.
5720: 
5721: 8
5722: 
5723: Archaeology, Gender and Identity
5724: 
5725: In the first half of this book, I have tried to present some of the
5726: historical background and content of current debates within archaeology.
5727: I have tended to present this background as a series of distinct
5728: traditions, drawing on distinct strands of social and cultural theory in
5729: general and having distinct and sometimes contradictory notions of where
5730: archaeology is or should be going.
5731: 
5732: In the process, I have had to over-simplify. I have presented these
5733: different traditions as both unitary and irrevocably opposed to one
5734: another: that all culture historians, processualists or
5735: postprocessualists think or thought this or that. The reader could be
5736: forgiven for thinking that the bars at archaeological conferences have
5737: lines of empty tables running down the middle of them separating
5738: distinct huddles, one muttering into their beer about cultural
5739: evolution, taphonomy and middle-range theory, the other getting agitated
5740: about hermeneutics, structuration theory and political commitment, while
5741: the culture historians have not even seen the need to turn up.
5742: 
5743: To put it another way, I have taken a very diverse and multi-stranded
5744: picture of archaeological thought, and have reified elements into
5745: distinct traditions. In other words, quite diffuse currents of thought
5746: have been converted mentally into definite things. Theoretical
5747: tendencies or interests have been presented as unified schools with a
5748: definite existence and autonomy of their own. Such a reification is, I
5749: suggest, a necessary evil, to introduce different strands of theory to
5750: the reader.
5751: 
5752: When explored in more depth, however, theoretical practice is much more
5753: varied and diffuse than this – just as archaeological practice is also
5754: much more varied and diffuse than many practical textbooks might
5755: indicate. We can use the analogy of political parties. I could write a
5756: description of what the British Conservative Party stands for, the
5757: history, intellectual traditions of and influences on Conservatives, and
5758: list ten or twelve points of current policy. But this is not to say that
5759: all individual Conservatives have the same beliefs or agree on the same
5760: policies.
5761: 
5762: First, as I stated in the Introduction, this book is very much a
5763: portrayal of theory that is structured around debates in the
5764: English-speaking world. Other traditions thrive in other areas of the
5765: globe – for example Latin American social archaeology, other strands of
5766: Marxist theory, different traditions of culture history in central and
5767: eastern Europe.
5768: 
5769: Second, the depiction of radically different traditions engaging in
5770: battle, in particular the processual/postprocessual divide, is rather
5771: out of date for the theoretical scene after 2000 if not earlier. Two
5772: decades of work since 1990 have, in different ways, combined elements of
5773: different traditions. As we have seen, Colin Renfrew has asserted that a
5774: broadly ‘scientific’ framework can be combined with a recognition that
5775: cognition is important and scientifically accessible. Tim Earle,
5776: Elizabeth Brumfiel and others have combined elements of cultural
5777: evolution and a generalizing perspective with a stress on competition,
5778: conflict, gender, and so on, as I discussed in chapter 5 and will return
5779: to in the next chapter. This ‘softening’ of processual thinking may be
5780: in response to postprocessual criticisms, or it may have happened anyway
5781: as part of a more general move within the human sciences. Or it may have
5782: happened as a result of practical necessities – even the most hardline
5783: positivist now has to deal with the legal and ethical requirements of
5784: dealing with Indigenous peoples or different elements of the community
5785: as part of the research process, particularly in contexts like the
5786: United States and Australia. However it happened, whatever route was
5787: taken, the destination is the same.
5788: 
5789: Some have talked of a consensus in theory, or of traditions at two
5790: extreme ends of a spectrum within which there is a comfortable middle
5791: position. I feel that ‘consensus’ is often asserted as an
5792: oversimplification, borne of an impatience, a desire to take a
5793: theoretical short-cut, to seek out a middle ground without necessarily
5794: putting in the hard theoretical labour needed to understand and
5795: critically evaluate different sides of the debate in the first place.
5796: There is also the point that a ‘spectrum’ between different positions is
5797: necessarily one-dimensional; there may be other dimensions or tensions
5798: which are glossed over or ignored. Nevertheless comments like the
5799: following are now commonplace to the point of orthodoxy within
5800: Anglo-American circles, that combine generalizing, ‘processual’ comments
5801: with explicit attention to meaning and context:
5802: 
5803: Goods and ideas moving among societies had variable significance in the
5804: groups to which they were introduced … what may have been a utilitarian
5805: item near its source could serve as a badge of office within a more
5806: distant locale. … In reconstructing interregional networks, we must be
5807: able to place the objects and styles which define them within their
5808: original behavioural contexts. Treating them as the undifferentiated
5809: products of monolithic, homogeneous cultures will only obscure the
5810: socio-political significance of ancient transactions. … Determining the
5811: behavioural significance of objects and styles invariably depends on
5812: careful analyses of their contexts of recovery. (Schortman and Urban
5813: 1992: 237)
5814: 
5815: Third, there are contemporary movements and interests that cut across
5816: the battle lines of previous traditions. Recent work, for example, in
5817: feminist archaeology and the archaeology of gender, has been done from a
5818: variety of theoretical frameworks. And interests common to many
5819: archaeologists in theoretical issues such as ‘space’, ‘agency’, and
5820: ‘trade and exchange’ cut across the divisions presented here. In the
5821: Conclusion, I will look at how some of these themes bring archaeologists
5822: of different theoretical orientations together in sometimes surprising
5823: ways. In this sense, the current state of ‘archaeological theory’ might
5824: be better characterized as a complex mosaic or changing kaleidoscope of
5825: themes and interests rather than as a titanic clash of different
5826: schools.
5827: 
5828: Fourth, the view taken in previous chapters presents different
5829: traditions as competing with one another. This was often the way that
5830: New and postprocessual archaeologies presented themselves in the past.
5831: But different traditions may be as much complementary as contradictory.
5832: For Robert Preucel (personal communication) ‘theory is best understood
5833: not as a succession of different theories, with each new theory
5834: superseding the previously favoured theory, but rather as a web of
5835: interrelated theories and approaches, with each constraining and
5836: enabling the others.’
5837: 
5838: Preucel is right: but there are, I feel, major contradictions between
5839: different theoretical views of the way the world works which are not
5840: easily resolved. It is very tempting to gloss over these differences,
5841: since we all like to feel that our own view occupies the ‘middle
5842: ground’, combines the best elements of all traditions, and that it is
5843: everybody else who is being ‘extreme’. Archaeologists, like politicians,
5844: like to be seen to be occupying the middle ground whatever the reality
5845: is. It is equally difficult to oppose the seductive argument that each
5846: theory has its own legitimate place within the grand scheme of things,
5847: with its overtones of tolerance, moderation and ‘fair play’. Ironically,
5848: the search for such a ‘middle ground’ carries the risk of its own
5849: disabling relativism – a superficially tolerant view in which no theory
5850: is better or worse than any other.
5851: 
5852: To get across some of the variety (and intellectual vitality) of this
5853: activity I want to use the next few chapters to look at different
5854: theoretical approaches to a number of current themes. All these issues
5855: cut across the theoretical schema laid out before; I could have written
5856: a book with politics, gender, history, evolution as the core themes, and
5857: added chapters to these defining different overall traditions.
5858: 
5859: Gender
5860: 
5861: One of the most popular themes in archaeological theory is that of an
5862: archaeology of gender. This interest has become widespread and overt
5863: since the early 1980s. As with other interests, this rise of an explicit
5864: concern has been paralleled by a growing interest in gender issues in
5865: virtually every other discipline in the humanities and human sciences,
5866: particularly sociology, literature, anthropology and history. It has
5867: also gone hand-in-hand with the growth of the feminist movement and
5868: feminist theory generally, though archaeological thought has lagged
5869: behind these other areas somewhat.
5870: 
5871: The archaeology of gender encompasses several different themes. These
5872: include: correction of male bias in archaeology; a critique of existing
5873: structures of archaeological practice; a reassessment of the history of
5874: archaeology; an examination of gender in the archaeological record; and
5875: a critique of what is seen as the male-biased nature of academic
5876: knowledge and the academic world in general. These different interests
5877: have, to an extent, succeeded and supplemented one another
5878: chronologically since the early 1980s. I have also listed them in a very
5879: rough order, from less to more controversial.
5880: 
5881: Bias Correction
5882: 
5883: Interest in the archaeology of gender started, in part, with a critique
5884: of androcentric assumptions. Androcentrism is the belief that men are at
5885: the centre of things, either making up society exclusively or with women
5886: on the margins.
5887: 
5888: The most obvious example is what feminists see as the sexist use of
5889: language – the use of ‘Man’ for human, or of ‘he’ when ‘she or he’ is
5890: meant. Consider the following quotes, collated by Fiona Burtt (1987)
5891: from a study of children’s books:
5892: 
5893: The favourite subjects of prehistoric artists seem to have been animals
5894: and women. This is quite logical as both were indispensable to
5895: prehistoric man (just as they are both indispensable to 20th century
5896: man). Animals guaranteed that he survived from day to day and women that
5897: he survived from generation to generation. (Mitchell 1981: 31)
5898: 
5899: Early man made a home in a cave. … He made scrapers and bones. … His
5900: wife used the scraper to clean the underside of animal skins. (Unstead
5901: 1953: 7; see also figure 8.1)
5902: 
5903: Or consider the following example, more ‘academic’, rather less obvious
5904: and as a result more difficult to disentangle, given as an explanation
5905: of the shift from the medieval castle to the Renaissance country house
5906: in England, made yet again by an avowedly atheoretical writer:
5907: 
5908: In the late Middle Ages … the individual was left by default to
5909: establish his own position and maintain his own security by means of a
5910: personal affinity of retainers and a public display of strength. From
5911: the late 15th century onwards, however … states that were rapidly
5912: growing in authority were increasingly able to circumscribe the power of
5913: the individual at the same time better able to guarantee his freedom
5914: within strictly defined boundaries … the individual is left to make good
5915: his claims to status and authority not only through the exercise of
5916: powers bestowed on him by the state but by the cultivation of more
5917: personal distinctions. (Cooper 1997: 120)
5918: 
5919: What is missing from this passage? Is ‘the individual’ here exclusively
5920: male, or does it include women as well? (Note the ‘as well’ that the
5921: reader is seamlessly drawn into). The author might reasonably expect his
5922: reader to ‘know’ (or simply to assume) that this society was largely
5923: patriarchal, and therefore he is referring largely to men when he talks
5924: of the individual. But then again, a minority of housebuilders of the
5925: period were women; the reader of the passage either mentally defines
5926: such women out of existence, treats them as surrogate men with exactly
5927: the same ideas and attitudes, or assumes they were ‘exceptions’ in some
5928: sense. What were women’s views on this ‘freedom’ in a period when many
5929: of them were regularly beaten with the sanction of the state and in
5930: which they were considered in law, politics and religion to be inferior
5931: to men? The passage does not tell us. Read the passage again, and form a
5932: picture of the individual in your mind, and I predict that you will find
5933: that it is a man.
5934: 
5935: Figure 8.1 Prehistoric life according to children’s books. From Unstead
5936: (1953: 20)
5937: 
5938: [c08_image001.jpg]
5939: 
5940: By leaving women out of such discourses, any attempt to ‘put them back’
5941: has to involve a lot of work: a complete rewriting of established
5942: commonsense terms and turns of phrase such as ‘public display’, ‘power’,
5943: ‘authority’. These terms which at first reading seemed obvious now
5944: appear rather opaque. It therefore becomes very difficult to place women
5945: ‘back into the picture’; subconsciously, we are disinclined to make the
5946: effort, particularly as the passage is written so apparently lucidly and
5947: smoothly. (It also becomes difficult to put men below the level of the
5948: elite, or children, back into the picture: a point we will return to
5949: towards the end of this chapter.) Women therefore become a ‘problem’;
5950: the literature is full of discussions of the methodological difficulties
5951: of interpreting women’s thoughts and activities during this period. But
5952: the ‘problem’ has in fact been created at least in part by a
5953: pre-existing discourse that is taken for granted. (It has also been
5954: created by reliance on a particular mode of historical narrative, a
5955: point discussed in chapter 11.)
5956: 
5957: Feminists argue that it is important to isolate these androcentric
5958: assumptions because when someone tells you that, for example, male
5959: dominance is normal or natural to the human species, they refer
5960: implicitly to the way males have always been – in the distant past, or
5961: in the primate and animal world. But this argument, they suggest, is
5962: circular. When we look at ‘empirical’ archaeological studies of the
5963: past, or ethnographic studies of other cultures, or anthropological
5964: studies of primates, we find that they have been studied from a
5965: viewpoint biased in favour of the male of the species. This bias is all
5966: the more pervasive because it is often unconscious.
5967: 
5968: Ethnographic studies are a good example. Various cross-cultural studies
5969: have suggested that ‘male dominance’ is universal. But consider for a
5970: moment the nature of this information. Much of it was collected in the
5971: nineteenth century by predominantly male ethnographers with literally
5972: Victorian attitudes towards such issues. Ethnographers would ‘naturally’
5973: choose to ask the men of the tribe about the nature of its political
5974: system rather than the women, and tend to interpret the varying and
5975: ambiguous replies that they got back in terms of their own Victorian
5976: expectations and preconceptions. Therefore, feminist anthropologists
5977: argue that such information derived from ethnography needs to be
5978: questioned and theorized rather than accepted blindly and uncritically.
5979: The same point goes for what we ‘know’ from other disciplines.
5980: 
5981: Another example of androcentrism is the history of archaeology itself.
5982: Read many traditional textbooks and you will find that archaeology
5983: developed via the discoveries and intellectual insights of, in the main,
5984: ‘great men’. Many have argued, however, that the contribution of women
5985: to archaeological thought has been systematically minimized by
5986: historians of archaeology. Figures such as Dorothy Garrod or Jacquetta
5987: Hawkes tend to be left out of many such narratives, or treated as
5988: somehow less important. An important recent strand of feminist
5989: archaeology has consisted of the rediscovery and rewriting of the
5990: history of archaeology to reflect the achievements of these women. The
5991: motivations for this rewriting may include a simple desire to ‘tell the
5992: past as it really was’, or to provide an inspiration for young women
5993: archaeologists in the present. It is striking that in response to this
5994: critique, successive editions of standard textbooks have been heavily
5995: modified (compare, for example, successive editions of Willey and
5996: Sabloff’s A History of American Archaeology, or the first and most
5997: recent editions of Renfrew and Bahn (2008)).
5998: 
5999: Critique of Archaeological Practice
6000: 
6001: Feminist archaeologists question the position of women within the
6002: archaeological profession. Overt and implicit sexism in recruitment
6003: policies, academic funding and promotion have all been interrogated,
6004: very much in line with the general questioning of discriminatory
6005: practices in Britain and North America generally.
6006: 
6007: For example, Joan Gero analysed the granting of research funds to
6008: different people. She found that men were statistically more successful
6009: at getting grants for excavation and ‘fieldwork’ in general, whereas
6010: women were statistically less likely to be awarded financial help, and
6011: more likely to be awarded grants for what Gero termed ‘archaeological
6012: housework’ – pottery and small finds analysis, environmental material.
6013: Was it then any surprise, she asked, when the interpretations produced
6014: by this fieldwork emphasized stereotypical images?
6015: 
6016: It is important to note that many of what Alison Wylie calls these
6017: ‘chilly-making activities’ are not dependent on overt bias, openly
6018: chauvinist attitudes or political discrimination as such, but are
6019: dependent on deeper and less obvious practices such as the use of
6020: language, hidden assumptions about career trajectories, even patterns of
6021: writing and conversation. This observation has two implications. First,
6022: it can be argued that scholars all participate in sexist practices
6023: however consciously they oppose sexism in principle, particularly if
6024: they are hostile or resistant to open reflection on their conduct (‘it’s
6025: just manners, it’s just a joke, it shouldn’t be taken seriously, it’s
6026: political correctness gone mad’). Second, the minutiae of everyday
6027: behaviour (how he is spoken of as assertive but she is spoken of as
6028: aggressive in the coffee room, the way we choose to dress for work)
6029: links in to wider practices – ‘the personal is political’.
6030: 
6031: Much of these first two points is fairly uncontroversial – few dispute
6032: the fact that past interpretations have reflected the conscious and
6033: unconscious sexism of their time, or that women continue to be
6034: discriminated against in contemporary society in a variety of ways, and
6035: that career development in archaeology reflects this. The next two
6036: points move on to more disputed territory.
6037: 
6038: Archaeologies of Gender
6039: 
6040: The third point is a desire to look at different constructions of gender
6041: in the past, as revealed in the archaeological record. If archaeologists
6042: think it is legitimate to take societies long extinct and look at
6043: ‘social ranking’, at trade and exchange, even at cognitive factors, why
6044: can’t we also look at different gender roles in the past?
6045: 
6046: These gender roles, it is argued, vary from culture to culture. A
6047: theoretical distinction is made between sex and gender. Sex is, it is
6048: argued, biological – most of us are born men or women, and our
6049: biological makeup does not vary (though even this can be questioned; see
6050: below).
6051: 
6052: There is a difference, however, between being born a biological male or
6053: female, and the experience of being ‘gendered’, of the experience and
6054: cultural practices of being a woman or a man in a given society. It can
6055: be argued that there is nothing ‘normal’ or ‘natural’ about acting like
6056: a man or a woman. For example, it was considered manly in the Middle
6057: Ages for élite men to weep and swoon on a regular basis; by the
6058: nineteenth century, the women were doing the weeping and swooning while
6059: the men were keeping a stiff upper lip. In some cultures women are
6060: considered sexually assertive and men are expected to be passive; and so
6061: on.
6062: 
6063: Gender, then, is culturally constructed, even if we accept the argument
6064: that gender is usually assigned on a biological basis. Gender varies
6065: from culture to culture. There is much debate about the extent of this
6066: variation (is it completely variable? are there some cross-cultural
6067: universals, for example to do with ‘domestic’ and childcare activities?)
6068: and how closely gender and sex are linked.
6069: 
6070: It follows that if archaeologists are interested in the past, we cannot
6071: assume that women and men behaved in the same way in all societies. For
6072: example, we cannot assume distinctions that we often take for granted
6073: existed in the past. These include ‘common-sense’ distinctions between
6074: domestic and public, between hunting and gathering, between the
6075: household and the wider world.
6076: 
6077: Instead, archaeologists need to ask questions about the different roles
6078: and experiences of women and men in a given period. These roles and
6079: experiences may have been similar or different; but the question needs
6080: to be asked rather than particular kinds of division assumed. Indeed, we
6081: need to question the assumption often made that a rigid binary division
6082: of labour (‘the men did this and the women did that’) existed at all.
6083: 
6084: Many argue that in practice all archaeology is ‘gendered’. They point
6085: out that many archaeologists make assumptions about gender in their
6086: work, as we pointed out above, and that there is no such thing as a
6087: ‘gender-neutral’ account of the past. Think of the quotes given above,
6088: or the way, for example, different human activities are valued. There
6089: are countless descriptions of craft specialization in the literature,
6090: but few of changing methods of food preparation. There is, feminists
6091: argue, an implicit assumption in much of the traditional literature that
6092: specialized craft activity was done by men and is therefore important,
6093: and food preparation was done by women and is less so. Food preparation
6094: may or may not have been performed by men, women or both; whoever
6095: prepared the food, it is certainly important in the constitution of
6096: culture.
6097: 
6098: An archaeology of gender, then, has led in practice to a re-examination
6099: of the small scale such as the archaeology of the household, and the
6100: reinterpretation of the domestic as an important part of social and
6101: political life in the past rather than simply a ‘domestic retreat’.
6102: 
6103: Men, Women and Knowledge
6104: 
6105: The phallocentric nature of knowledge is a difficult point to grasp,
6106: since it involves questioning the very basis of academic enquiry, or
6107: indeed what it is to be a student or a teacher.
6108: 
6109: Think about the way academics praise an argument, or denigrate it. We
6110: praise ‘strong’, ‘robust’, ‘well organized’, ‘powerful’ arguments;
6111: conversely, we denigrate arguments that are perceived as weak, woolly,
6112: tentative, inconclusive. The language of archaeological theory itself
6113: can often be the language of battle – theories conflict and clash,
6114: different schools muster allies to strengthen their arguments. Debate is
6115: adversarial and confrontational: one side gets the better or the worse
6116: of the ‘argument’. Students are taught to be impersonal in their essays
6117: – to separate emotions and feelings from academic argument, to omit
6118: personal experience from their writing.
6119: 
6120: Many feminists argue that the academic structuring of knowledge in this
6121: way is phallocentric. That is, the academic establishment treats
6122: stereotypically ‘male’ ways of thinking and acting as legitimate, and
6123: stereotypically ‘female’ ways as illegitimate, ‘out of court’.
6124: Phallocentric discourse claims to be fair and neutral, to offer a ‘level
6125: playing field’; the professor will tell you that anyone can write a
6126: first class paper if they follow the correct academic rules and have the
6127: necessary commitment and intellectual ability. In practice, however, the
6128: rules of this game are discriminatory, since (among other reasons) it
6129: requires women to think and write ‘like men’, at least stereotypically,
6130: if they are to succeed. If men have been socialized into this way of
6131: thinking from birth, the argument continues, is it any surprise that
6132: they are ‘better’ at this academic game than women?
6133: 
6134: What is needed, feminists suggest, are different rules for the game. Why
6135: can’t we be ‘emotional’ when writing? What is wrong with multivocality –
6136: telling stories with different voices, different conclusions? Why can’t
6137: we write stories about people’s personal experiences? What’s wrong with
6138: ending one’s presentation with a question rather than a final, firm
6139: conclusive statement?
6140: 
6141: In this way, much feminist critique ends up at a very similar position
6142: to that of many postprocessual archaeologists; indeed, many of the
6143: postprocessual arguments rehearsed in chapter 5 owe a profound debt to
6144: feminist thinking on this and other points (see below).
6145: 
6146: Case Study: What This Awl Means
6147: 
6148: Janet Spector’s book What This Awl Means: Feminist Archaeology at a
6149: Wahpeton Dakota Village is a study of a nineteenth-century Native
6150: American settlement, Little Rapids, in what is now Minnesota in the
6151: Midwestern USA. Rather than attempt to write a neutral, distanced,
6152: objective site report, however, Spector consciously and explicitly sets
6153: out to explore her original, emotive reasons for wanting to be an
6154: archaeologist: ‘these motives are empathetic – a longing to discover
6155: essences, images, and feelings of the past – not detached, distanced,
6156: objective’ (Spector 1993: 1).
6157: 
6158: Spector breaks three rules of academic discourse in the opening
6159: chapters. First, she goes into her own past life history and explores
6160: her personal reasons for doing archaeology and coming to the Little
6161: Rapids site. Second, rather than looking at ‘big questions’ such as
6162: social complexity or the pattern of trade between Native and white
6163: Americans, she focuses initially on one very tiny artefact, apparently
6164: insignificant in itself – ‘a small antler awl handle’ (figure 8.2).
6165: Third, Spector writes a story about this handle – how it was lost, what
6166: the young woman who owned it was doing at the time, what the experience
6167: of life at Little Rapids was like. The story is backed up by Spector’s
6168: extensive knowledge and understanding of the archaeological and historic
6169: data, but is self-consciously written as a story nevertheless, intended
6170: to evoke the emotions and spirit of the inhabitants of the site.
6171: 
6172: Spector contrasts the story she has told with previous archaeological
6173: work on awls. Previous typologies of awl handles present themselves as
6174: neutral classifications, Spector argues, but they are in fact biased in
6175: respect both to ethnicity and gender. Conventional typologies treat awls
6176: first and foremost as trade goods brought in by Europeans, rather than
6177: as artefacts related to Native American (‘Indian’) women’s activities:
6178: 
6179: An important but hidden assumption … is that European-produced metal awl
6180: tips are more important than Indian-produced awl handles. Built into
6181: [these] classifications and table titles, this theme leads to emphasis
6182: on metal awl tips as markers of European influence on Indians and
6183: implies the disintegration of native culture. This would have been
6184: insulting, annoying, or simply wrong to Indians who used awls,
6185: particularly to women who inscribed their bone or antler handles to
6186: display publicly their accomplishments. (Spector 1993: 31–2)
6187: 
6188: Figure 8.2 The awl handle excavated by Spector’s crew. Spector’s
6189: narrative links it to the young girl Mazaokeyiwin who lived at Little
6190: Rapids and may have lost the awl in the 1840s (Spector 1993)
6191: 
6192: [c08_image002.jpg]
6193: 
6194: Figure 8.3 The elderly Mazaokeyiwin working a hide (Spector 1993)
6195: 
6196: [c08_image003.jpg]
6197: 
6198: Spector tells stories also about the progress of the dig, the discovery
6199: of the awl, the experiences of different dig members, and frankly
6200: explores tensions and cooperation with Native Americans in the course of
6201: the research project. She is helped in her task by the extensive
6202: historical and ethnographic documentation of the Dakota people and of
6203: the site, that allows her to give names and personalities to many of the
6204: nineteenth-century inhabitants of Little Rapids (figure 8.3).
6205: 
6206: It is striking that Spector’s openly feminist perspective leads her to
6207: accentuate the small scale and personal (what the awl handle meant to
6208: its owner, not the awl’s position within wider trade networks). It is
6209: laid out in a personal and engaged way (the personalities and excitement
6210: of the research project) rather than attempting to be rhetorically
6211: dispassionate. It leads very quickly to other dimensions of social and
6212: cultural identity (the transition from being a girl to being an adult
6213: woman in Dakota society). It is also striking how Spector’s feminist
6214: account is also immediately and unavoidably engaged in other questions
6215: of politics and identity, most obviously that of relations between
6216: Native Americans and whites.
6217: 
6218: This all sounds fine, but I’m getting a little confused over terminology
6219: here. Is gender archaeology the same thing as feminist archaeology?
6220: 
6221: Not necessarily. It is without question that interest in gender issues
6222: in archaeology grew out of the rise in feminist concerns since the
6223: 1960s. But remember that it is pretty undeniable to assert that all
6224: archaeology makes assumptions about gender, unless it forswears any
6225: attempt at social reconstruction. If we need to look at gender in the
6226: past, there is no a priori reason why a specifically feminist
6227: perspective needs to be used.
6228: 
6229: Conkey and Gero (1997) argue that the archaeology of gender is
6230: increasingly diverse in the theoretical positions it has taken. They
6231: show that sociobiology, social constructivism, cultural and biological
6232: evolution, political economy, and agency and performance theory have all
6233: been used to structure enquiry into gender in the archaeological record.
6234: They point to the very deep differences in theoretical assumptions
6235: behind many of these schools, and assert that many studies of gender do
6236: not draw extensively or even at all on feminist critiques within the
6237: human sciences in general.
6238: 
6239: Of course, it all depends on definitions of feminism, which vary. ‘I
6240: myself have never been able to find out precisely what feminism is: I
6241: only know that people call me a feminist whenever I express sentiments
6242: that differentiate me from a doormat or a prostitute’ (Rebecca West
6243: 1913; cited in Humm 1992: 34). More specifically, Conkey and Gero draw
6244: on feminist critiques of science and point to four areas that they feel
6245: distinguish specifically feminist enquiry in archaeology:
6246: 
6247: 1 The recognition that ‘politics and the substantive products of
6248: knowledge are essentially inseparable’;
6249: 
6250: 2 The recognition that rationality is a ‘mythical conflation that never
6251: exists in actual scientific practice’;
