6252: 
6253: 3 ‘Association with a cognitive style that favours “intimate” knowledge
6254: and nuanced understandings of data over categorical thinking’;
6255: 
6256: 4 A challenge to ‘basic disciplinary arrangements’ and the fostering of
6257: alternative views. (Conkey and Gero 1997: 427–8)
6258: 
6259: Conkey and Gero argue that feminist archaeology is, or should be, a
6260: dangerous and transforming exercise. They distance themselves from
6261: considerations ‘of gender in prehistoric studies without reconfiguring
6262: archaeology in any way’ and assert that
6263: 
6264: An archaeology that takes feminist theory seriously is
6265: self-transformational and communal. Radical reappraisals – rigorous,
6266: scholarly, informed, and purposive – emerge from feminist theory
6267: precisely because traditional assumptions and values really do look
6268: profoundly different when viewed from a womancentred perspective. Some
6269: have wanted to call this ‘seeing gender everywhere’, with the derogatory
6270: term ‘genderlirium’. But ‘genderlirium’ is an equally apt term with
6271: which to critique Western androcentrism, with its hard-headed rules for
6272: a single way of knowing and its single vision. (Conkey and Gero 1997:
6273: 424, 430)
6274: 
6275: For Conkey and Gero, then, archaeological studies of gender come from a
6276: variety of theoretical viewpoints, but a specifically feminist
6277: standpoint is one that questions existing archaeological practice at a
6278: very basic level.
6279: 
6280: OK, so we can’t assume men and women did this or that in the past. But
6281: in that case how can we ever begin to talk about gender?
6282: 
6283: This impinges on the question of essentialism. In the view of some
6284: feminists, there is nothing essential to ‘feminine nature’. Some attack
6285: ‘biological essentialism’, that is, the attempt to define
6286: cross-culturally gender roles based on biological ‘facts’ such as men’s
6287: greater upper-body strength or women’s role in childbirth and infant
6288: care. They argue that such ‘facts’ are socially constructed. For
6289: example, they question the way gender differences are always presented
6290: in a binary fashion, rather than as a continuum, in biological studies.
6291: 
6292: The problem you raise is that if we cannot say with certainty which
6293: activities were undertaken by which gender, or even that activities were
6294: or were not gendered, then we cannot say anything positive about the
6295: construction of gender in a given society, particularly a prehistoric
6296: one.
6297: 
6298: One way out of this that is frequently taken is to construct a
6299: contextual argument. For example, one can look at which artefacts are
6300: associated with which gender in burials, and then extend this argument
6301: outwards, to look at the same classes of artefacts in domestic contexts.
6302: Another way out is to rely on a limited essentialism: for example, that
6303: the necessities of childrearing do involve an association of women with
6304: domestic and household contexts.
6305: 
6306: You’ve said that an interest in gender cuts across previous theoretical
6307: categories. But it seems to me that it’s closely related to
6308: postprocessual archaeology. Both movements relate archaeology to
6309: politics, both try to look at conflict and inequality, both try to write
6310: different narratives that include the personal…
6311: 
6312: Historically, the archaeology of gender and postprocessual archaeology
6313: have close links both with each other and with feminist thought in the
6314: human sciences. Many of the younger archaeologists in the 1980s and
6315: 1990s were closely involved with both movements, particularly in
6316: Britain. Specifically, we can point to: (a) the widening of the
6317: definition of ‘political’; (b) stress on small-scale, local and
6318: household contexts as important; (c) stress on multivocality; (d) stress
6319: on meaning and lived experience; and (e) stress on conflict as being
6320: played out through everyday practice. Indeed, I suspect that when the
6321: history of postprocessual thought in the 1980s comes to be written it
6322: will be found that the role of individual feminists has been
6323: under-acknowledged.
6324: 
6325: But many would argue that bias correction (point 1 above) is a
6326: legitimate and indeed essential part of any scientific archaeology.
6327: Within science as a whole, much work has been done to correct what are
6328: seen as specific biases; for example, critiques of Charles Darwin’s
6329: terminology when he attributes ‘passive’ and ‘active’ roles to female
6330: and male apes respectively.
6331: 
6332: Similarly, there is no necessary conflict between a positivist view of
6333: science and stress on recruitment and equal rights issues within
6334: archaeology. It is perfectly possible to argue for ‘scientific method’
6335: in archaeological reasoning, while acknowledging that the archaeological
6336: profession needs to address issues of discrimination. But in accordance
6337: with positivist method, one’s stance on ‘political’ issues such as
6338: equality should be kept separate from one’s academic judgements.
6339: 
6340: Some feminists, particularly in the human sciences as a whole, argue
6341: strongly against what they see as the slippery nature of the way some
6342: arguments under the postprocessual banner see everything in a very
6343: multifaceted, deliberately ambiguous way, in which the very real
6344: powerlessness and exploitation felt by many women seems to them to be
6345: glossed over. Isn’t it ironic, they point out, that just as women are
6346: beginning to get a small slice of the cake, making a real difference at
6347: work and so on, that it’s revealed that such bread-and-butter issues are
6348: only a ‘language game’ anyway? Alison Wylie writes that:
6349: 
6350: strong constructivist and relativist positions embody what seem to be
6351: patently an ideology of the powerful. Only the most powerful, the most
6352: successful in achieving control over the world, could be constructed as
6353: they choose. … Any who lack such power, or who lack an investment in
6354: believing they have such power, are painfully aware that they negotiate
6355: an intransigent reality that impinges on their lives at every turn.
6356: (Wylie 1992b: 25)
6357: 
6358: A related argument is that of inclusiveness. Feminists often feel that
6359: their arguments are ‘hijacked’, made part of some ‘wider argument’ such
6360: as that of Marxism or of postprocessual archaeology and in the process
6361: subsumed and ultimately ignored. It is striking that Spector prefers her
6362: argument to stand on its own two feet. Her bibliography refers to very
6363: little of the ‘postprocessual’ literature. In practical terms, just as
6364: the majority of New Archaeologists were pushy young men, so some
6365: feminists argue that postprocessualism was a distinctly male phenomenon
6366: in its early years, with an aggressive and uncompromising tone to its
6367: rhetoric; many men initially associated with postprocessual ideas have
6368: now slid seamlessly into senior academic positions.
6369: 
6370: Archaeologies of Identity
6371: 
6372: We noted above how Spector’s argument about gender led very quickly into
6373: other issues of social identity – for example, of age, of ethnicity.
6374: They also led very quickly into an attempt to write in a different way
6375: about the past, a way that was engaged and emotive rather than detached
6376: and ‘purely objective’. What this demonstrates is that an archaeology of
6377: gender leads inevitably into a number of other areas and dimensions of
6378: social identity.
6379: 
6380: ‘Archaeologies of identity’ take their lead from gender and feminist
6381: archaeology, in that they all take an interest in how social identities
6382: are constructed, how those identities might be explored in the
6383: archaeological record, and how identity formation itself is always
6384: ‘socially constituted’ – in other words, it is variable, unstable, fluid
6385: and changing, rather than being fixed or essential. Archaeologists have
6386: explored, among other areas, archaeologies of masculinity, of childhood,
6387: of race, of sexuality, and of disability.
6388: 
6389: The first and most obvious of these constructions is masculinity. If the
6390: roles and identities of women were variable in the past, then it follows
6391: that men’s were too. An archaeology of masculinity and of men’s changing
6392: identities is therefore a necessary part of any full understanding of
6393: gender.
6394: 
6395: For example, in my own work, discussed in chapter 11, I have looked at
6396: how medieval castles, and their transformation into Renaissance palaces,
6397: was tied up with changing notions of élite masculinity – what it meant
6398: to be a knight or a gent. In the later middle ages, to be a man was
6399: closely associated with the ability and training to fight. Masculinity
6400: was associated with values like honour and chivalry. Chivalry and
6401: martial values were given material expression in the towers and
6402: battlements of castles, in the labour invested in suits of armour, and
6403: in the ability to ride a specially bred warhorse and to use a lance and
6404: shield while on horseback, for example in the ritualized violence of
6405: jousting and the tournament. The ‘decline of the castle’ and the shift
6406: to an unfortified Renaissance architecture gave expression to new values
6407: of what it meant to be a man, a new ‘self-fashioning’, in which ideas of
6408: civility, of literacy and familiarity with the works of Classical
6409: literature, and of ‘courtly’ behaviour became much more important. I
6410: tried to draw out two points from this discussion: first, ‘what it meant
6411: to be a man’ was not the same in all times and in all places; second,
6412: these meanings were materially expressed in the appearance, layout and
6413: decorative schemes of buildings.
6414: 
6415: Again, Laurie Wilkie has looked at the architecture and material culture
6416: associated with men’s fraternity houses at the University of California
6417: at Berkeley at the beginning of the twentieth century. Wilkie looks at
6418: the ceramics and other evidence for dining practices, and the changing
6419: layout and locations of the fraternity houses. These young men, she
6420: argues, had their identities shaped around contemporary ideas of a
6421: ‘primal masculinity’ that was in part a reaction to changing women’s
6422: identities of the period.
6423: 
6424: If men’s identities were variable, so also ‘childhood’ has to be treated
6425: as a constructed category. Just as theorists made a distinction between
6426: sex and gender, so it can be argued that the physical process of growth
6427: and ageing can be separated analytically from the socially constructed
6428: experience of ‘childhood’. Ideas, for example, of when a child becomes
6429: an adult vary from culture to culture, as they do for old age. In
6430: pre-industrial England, many children were sent away from home to be
6431: servants in other’s households from an early age, giving a socially
6432: defined intermediate period that corresponded very roughly with
6433: adolescence. Jo Sofaer has explored different constructions of
6434: childhood, both through the treatment of children in burial practices
6435: and through analysis of aspects of the lives of children, such as
6436: nutrition and different activities, through human skeletal evidence.
6437: 
6438: Archaeologists have also rethought other dimensions of social identity
6439: such as race and ethnicity. It has long been accepted, since the work of
6440: Franz Boas and the rejection of racist theories of culture a century
6441: ago, that race is a socially constructed category, rather than a simple
6442: matter of DNA or skin colour. Since the time of Boas, social scientists
6443: have preferred to talk of ‘ethnicity’ on the basis of social and
6444: cultural rather than biological distinction. I have argued in earlier
6445: chapters that essentialist statements like ‘the Celtic spirit’ have the
6446: potential to be used in problematic ways that often seem to suggest or
6447: imply some kind of inner racial essence. In historical archaeology,
6448: changing constructions of African-American identity have been explored,
6449: looking, for example at an ‘archaeology of resistance’, how elements of
6450: African identities were maintained and transformed by enslaved peoples
6451: in the most oppressive of contexts. I will look in chapter 12 at the
6452: archaeology of the African Burial Ground in New York.
6453: 
6454: If ideas about gender are variable, the same also goes, it is argued,
6455: for ideas about sexuality. I wrote above that ‘sex’ can be argued to be
6456: biological, whereas gender is socially constructed. Inevitably, things
6457: are not as simple as this. Sexual identities were also variable in the
6458: past, and cannot be assumed in the archaeological record. It may be that
6459: there were other sexual identities in the past. An archaeology of
6460: sexuality, like the archaeology of gender, spans empirical enquiry (for
6461: example, drawing attention to depictions of same-sex relations in
6462: Swedish rock art or on Classical vases) to a theoretical reformulation
6463: of archaeology.
6464: 
6465: The project of ‘queer theory’ in the human sciences has led to the
6466: advocacy of ‘queer archaeologies’. Queer theory is a drawing-together of
6467: insights from gay and lesbian studies. If traditional scholars in the
6468: human sciences often make androcentric assumptions (assuming the male
6469: experience is normal and/or central in the past), they also make
6470: heterosexist assumptions (assuming heterosexual orientations or
6471: relationships were normal and/or central in the past). Queer
6472: archaeologies move from an identification of evidence for other forms of
6473: sexuality to a wider project of ‘queering the past’, just as feminist
6474: archaeologists move from identifying changing constructions of gender to
6475: an attempt to change archaeological practice.
6476: 
6477: Just as ideas in feminist archaeology mirrored discussions in other
6478: social sciences, so too have discussions of disability. Traditional
6479: accounts have either not considered disability, or have seen disabled
6480: people in the archaeological record as dependent on the able-bodied, and
6481: ‘care’ for disability as an index of civilized behaviour. The classic
6482: example is the famous interpretation of the Neanderthal individual
6483: ‘Shanidar I’. Summarizing Ralph Solecki’s arguments, Trinkaus and
6484: Shipman state that:
6485: 
6486: Careful study of his bones revealed a plethora of serious but healed
6487: fractures … someone so devastatingly injured could not possibly have
6488: survived without care and sustenance … a one-armed, partially blind,
6489: crippled man could have made no pretence of hunting or gathering his own
6490: food. That he survived for years after his trauma was a testament to
6491: Neandertal compassion and humanity. (Trinkaus and Shipman 1992, 340–1)
6492: 
6493: The point here is not that the interpretation of Shanidar I is ‘wrong’,
6494: but that it is one-sided. It follows older models of disability in
6495: seeing the individual exclusively in terms of what he could not do, as
6496: opposed to what he could do or might have been able to do, and in terms
6497: of the need for ‘compassion and humanity’ from (it is implied)
6498: able-bodied others. This individual’s survival might be a testament not
6499: simply or solely to ‘compassion’, but to this person’s many other skills
6500: and qualities that he brought to the social group. Ironically, it fell
6501: to the author Jean Auel in her novel Clan of the Cave Bear to use the
6502: evidence of Shanidar I to imagine Shanidar I as a shaman, a powerful
6503: figure, a person defined socially not simply in terms of his impairment
6504: but in terms of his magical power over the social and natural world.
6505: 
6506: In the human sciences generally, and in parallel with the distinction
6507: between sex and gender, scholars have drawn a distinction between
6508: ‘impairment’ and ‘disability’. Disability is, in this view, a social
6509: category or experience rather than the ‘objective’ medical condition of
6510: impairment. To take a classic example, wheelchair users have an
6511: impairment, but the social experience of disability is conditioned by,
6512: for example, the choice to use steps instead of ramps, the layout and
6513: design of buildings and pathways, all of which are commonly designed
6514: with the needs of the ‘able-bodied’ in – mind and by the unconscious and
6515: unconscious biases and assumptions of others (‘she can’t possibly do
6516: this’, ‘given his handicap, doesn’t he manage well’).
6517: 
6518: An archaeology of disability, then, spans a number of related themes:
6519: issues of access to archaeology as a profession, and workplace issues of
6520: disability, to an empirical investigation of the relation between
6521: impairment and social identities in the past.
6522: 
6523: Performativity
6524: 
6525: In line with discussions of gender, theorists in the social sciences
6526: have insisted that all these categories (gender, race, age, sexual
6527: identity, disability) are performative. ‘Performativity’ is a centrally
6528: important idea in understanding identities; it is particularly
6529: associated with the theorist Judith Butler. For example, what it means
6530: to be a man or a woman is only partly determined by biology, as
6531: discussed above. It is also determined, at least in part, by the daily
6532: performances men and women put on – their manner of speech, choice of
6533: dress, behaviour in social situations. These performances can be thought
6534: of as little pieces of theatre, even if the audience is one (the self).
6535: 
6536: There are four important implications for archaeological practice that
6537: arise from an appreciation of performativity. First, performance leads
6538: to a stress on the importance of material culture. Performances occur in
6539: material settings (the open halls of houses; structured stages like
6540: Roman parade grounds or Maya temples or Greek stoa). They frequently
6541: occur in relation to material objects and are defined through material
6542: use (dress, make-up). So performativity gives an important dimension to
6543: archaeologists’ appreciation of materiality and the nature of the
6544: material world.
6545: 
6546: Second, performances structure and define ‘who you are’. Further, they
6547: are manipulated by people. Gay and lesbian subcultures use dress and
6548: behaviour to redefine ‘what it means’ to be gay and lesbian. ‘What it
6549: means’ to be African, or British, or a citizen of the USA can change or
6550: be manipulated by performance – the choice of ‘traditional’ or Western
6551: clothes, the choice to celebrate chicken tikka masala rather than fish
6552: and chips as the nation’s favourite dish, to wear a Barack Obama T-shirt
6553: or a Stetson. Therefore, for archaeologists, material culture cannot be
6554: interpreted as the manifestation of some pre-existing essence – for
6555: example we cannot assert ‘who the Slavs were’, or talk of ‘middle-class
6556: identity’ or ways of life, and then look for their archaeological
6557: ‘expression’, or lack of expression, in the archaeological record. I
6558: will return to this point in discussing materiality in the Conclusion.
6559: 
6560: Third, performances often take place through the body. ‘The body’
6561: emerged as a key theme in current thinking when I discussed the ‘lived
6562: experience’ explored by phenomenologists. Here, the body is important
6563: because it too is a piece of material culture that can be manipulated.
6564: Much of the archaeological record is about different constructions of
6565: the body – most obviously with grave goods, but also ‘personal’ items
6566: like jewellery, grooming items such as combs and tweezers, and more
6567: broadly dress and other personal items. People’s bodies, then, become a
6568: key, even central, element of their performance.
6569: 
6570: Fourth, performativity applies just as much to contemporary practice as
6571: it does to interpretations of the past. Performances occur in the
6572: lecture theatre or classroom, or indeed on the archaeological
6573: excavation. The roles of professor and student, site director and finds
6574: assistant are just as open to a performative analysis as roles in the
6575: past. Therefore, as with feminism and Marxism, there is an important
6576: reflexive component to archaeologists’ thinking about performativity.
6577: Reflexivity refers to the back-and-forth, doubleedged nature of academic
6578: enquiry, in which what we learn about the past is always and immediately
6579: bound up with practices in the present (for example, where critique of
6580: past ideologies immediately reflects back on ideology in the present, or
6581: where critical thinking about past gender roles requires us to reflect
6582: on sexist assumptions in the workplace and in our own heads).
6583: 
6584: Conclusion
6585: 
6586: What all these approaches and themes share in common is:
6587: 
6588: -   a stress on identities as socially constructed;
6589: -   a scepticism towards the idea that identity is somehow ‘given’ by
6590:     some core or essential feature, for example through biological sex
6591:     or DNA;
6592: -   a stress on agency and performativity in the active creation of
6593:     identities;
6594: -   a belief that while these themes were initially explored in other
6595:     disciplines, archaeologists need to engage with them;
6596: -   a continuum or tension between ‘orthodox’ empirical studies of a
6597:     particular theme and a wholesale recasting of the rules of academic
6598:     enquiry;
6599: -   a stress on the connections between study of identities in the past
6600:     and identity politics in the present;
6601: -   a stress on the reflexive relationship between both the study of the
6602:     past and the present, and between academic study and the everyday
6603:     politics of the ‘real world’.
6604: 
6605: As we have seen, there is a tendency with each of these interests to
6606: style itself as a different sub-field or species or method of
6607: archaeology: thus ‘queer archaeologies’, ‘archaeology of sexuality’,
6608: ‘archaeology of disability’.
6609: 
6610: This proliferation of archaeologies of identity can be highly fruitful
6611: and enabling. It has the potential to make archaeology not just more
6612: sensitive to different aspects of identity in the past, but open and
6613: relevant to different elements of the community in the present. However,
6614: it can also be problematic for several reasons.
6615: 
6616: In the first place, it is possible to argue that a particular
6617: structuring principle is not just another -ism or another archaeology,
6618: but is in some sense primary. Most obviously, many feminists continue to
6619: assert gender as a primary structuring principle to human societies in
6620: the past, to place a feminist agenda as primary in the present, and to
6621: resist assimilation to a ‘wider project’ or classification as just one
6622: of a series of themes. Many such feminists would be sceptical of the
6623: presentation of an ‘archaeology of gender’ alongside other archaeologies
6624: of identity, as I have done here. Again, it is possible to argue that
6625: race is a fundamental structuring category, not just another dimension
6626: of identity to be set against others.
6627: 
6628: Second, some archaeologists are wary of what they fear will be an
6629: endless proliferation of ‘archaeologies’. If many different kinds of
6630: identity can be explored, and each has its own distinctive archaeology,
6631: it can lead paradoxically to the trivialization of all of them. Social
6632: theorists have criticized an unrestrained identity politics as leading
6633: to a position where people can just pick-and-mix different identities,
6634: like products off a supermarket shelf. Paradoxically, then, a
6635: proliferation of identities can be argued to be a feature of what
6636: Marxists call ‘late capitalism’. (It has to be said in reply, however,
6637: that archaeologists with such worries turn out, all too often, to be
6638: able-bodied white men.)
6639: 
6640: For me, the critical point is that current thinking in feminist and
6641: gender archaeology, and in archaeologies of identity, express some of
6642: the key tensions in current archaeological theory. They embody some of
6643: the difficulties, and potentials, of doing archaeology in a complex
6644: modern world. I will return to these difficulties and potentials in
6645: chapter 12.
6646: 
6647: 9
6648:
